volumeTitle: Antigone. The Women of Trachis. Philoctetes. Oedipus at Colonus
label: Philoctetes (Section 485 - Section 641)

sections.label: Section 485
sections.original: πείσθητι · προσπίτνω σε γόνασι , καίπερ ὢν
sections.translation: be persuaded ! I supplicate you at your knees , I am an infirm wretch , and lame ! Do not leave me desolate like this , far from the paths of mankind ! No , bring me safely to your own home , or to Euboea , Chalcodon ’ s seat ;

sections.label: Section 490
sections.original: κἀκεῖθεν οὔ μοι μακρὸς εἰς Οἴτην στόλος
sections.translation: and from there it will be no long journey for me to Oeta and the Trachinian heights , and fair-flowing Spercheius , so that you may show me to my beloved father , though long I have feared that he may have departed me . For often

sections.label: Section 495
sections.original: ἔστελλον αὐτὸν ἱκεσίους πέμπων λιτάς ,
sections.translation: did I summon him by means of those who came here , sending imploring prayers that he would himself send a ship and get me safely home . But either he is dead , or else , as I think is likely , my messengers thought my concerns of little account and hurried on their homeward voyage .

sections.label: Section 500
sections.original: νῦν δʼ , εἰς σὲ γὰρ πομπόν τε καὐτὸν ἄγγελον
sections.translation: Now , however , since in you I have found one who can be both an escort and a messenger , save me and show me mercy , keeping in mind that all human destiny is full of the fear and the danger that prosperity may be followed by its opposite . He who stands clear of trouble must beware of dangers ,

sections.label: Section 505
sections.original: χὤταν τις εὖ ζῇ , τηνικαῦτα τὸν βίον
sections.translation: and when a man lives at ease , then it is that he must look most closely to his livelihood , lest it secretly suffer ruin .

sections.label: Section 1.507
sections.original: οἴκτιρʼ , ἄναξ · πολλῶν ἔλεξεν δυσοίστων πόνων
sections.translation: Have pity , my king ! He has told of a struggle with sufferings manifold and oppressive — may the like befall no friend of mine !

sections.label: Section 1.510
sections.original: εἰ δὲ πικρούς , ἄναξ , ἔχθεις Ἀτρείδας ,
sections.translation: And if , my king , you hate the hateful Atreids , then , turning their wrongdoing to this man ’ s gain ,

sections.label: Section 1.515
sections.original: μετατιθέμενος , ἔνθαπερ ἐπιμέμονεν ,
sections.translation: I would take him in your swift , well-rigged ship to the home for which he longs , and so escape the god ’ s just wrath .

sections.label: Section 519
sections.original: ὅρα σὺ μὴ νῦν μέν τις εὐχερὴς παρῇς ,
sections.translation: Though now as a casual spectator you are compliant ,

sections.label: Section 520
sections.original: ὅταν δὲ πλησθῇς τῆς νόσου ξυνουσίᾳ ,
sections.translation: beware lest later , when filled with his disease by its constant company , you prove no longer constant to these sentiments .

sections.label: Section 522
sections.original: ἥκιστα · τοῦτʼ οὐκ ἔσθʼ ὅπως ποτʼ εἰς ἐμὲ
sections.translation: That will not happen . You will never have just cause to rebuke me for that !

sections.label: Section 524
sections.original: ἀλλʼ αἰσχρὰ μέντοι σοῦ γέ μʼ ἐνδεέστερον
sections.translation: Well , then , it would shame me if the stranger were to find

sections.label: Section 525
sections.original: ξένῳ φανῆναι πρὸς τὸ καίριον πονεῖν .
sections.translation: me less ready than you are to toil for his good . Come , if it pleases you , let us sail . Let the man set out at once ; our ship , for her part , will carry him , and will not refuse . Only may the gods give us safe passage from this land , and from here to whatever destination we choose !

sections.label: Section 530
sections.original: ὦ φίλτατον μὲν ἦμαρ , ἥδιστος δʼ ἀνήρ ,
sections.translation: O day of joy unsurpassed ! Most delightful man , and you good sailors ! If only I could show you in deeds what a true friend you have made in me ! Let us be going , my son , when we two have made a solemn farewell to my homeless home inside , so that you may also learn

sections.label: Section 535
sections.original: ἀφʼ ὧν διέζων ὥς τʼ ἔφυν εὐκάρδιος .
sections.translation: by what means I sustained my life , and how stout of heart I was born . For I believe that the mere sight would have deterred any other man but me from enduring these sufferings . But I have been slowly schooled by necessity to endure misery . Neoptolemus is about to follow Philoctetes into the cave .

sections.label: Section 539
sections.original: ἐπίσχετον , μάθωμεν · ἄνδρε γὰρ δύο ,
sections.translation: Wait , let us listen to the two men who are coming !

sections.label: Section 540
sections.original: ὁ μὲν νεὼς σῆς ναυβάτης , ὁ δʼ ἀλλόθρους ,
sections.translation: One is a crewman of your ship , the other a stranger . Go in after you hear their report .

sections.label: Section 542
sections.original: Ἀχιλλέως παῖ , τόνδε τὸν ξυνέμπορον ,
sections.translation: Son of Achilles , I asked my companion here , when he was guarding your ship with two others , to tell me where you might be found ,

sections.label: Section 545
sections.original: ἐπείπερ ἀντέκυρσα , δοξάζων μὲν οὔ ,
sections.translation: since I have chanced upon you unexpectedly by the good fortune of coming to anchor off this very coast . With no great company I am homeward bound on my trader ’ s voyage from Ilium to Peparethus with its cluster-laden vines , but when I heard that the sailors

sections.label: Section 550
sections.original: σοὶ πάντες εἶεν συννεναυστοληκότες ,
sections.translation: were all of your crew , I resolved not to continue my voyage in silence , without first giving you my news and getting the due reward . You know nothing , I suspect , of your own affairs : the new designs the Greeks

sections.label: Section 555
sections.original: βουλεύματʼ ἐστί , κοὐ μόνον βουλεύματα ,
sections.translation: have regarding you , and not only designs , but deeds in progress and no longer postponed .

sections.label: Section 557
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἡ χάρις μὲν τῆς προμηθίας , ξένε ,
sections.translation: The service you have done me by your forethought , sir , unless I am unworthy , will remain in my grateful thoughts . But tell me just what the designs are that you mentioned , so that I may understand

sections.label: Section 560
sections.original: νεώτερον , βούλευμʼ ἀπʼ Ἀργείων ἔχεις .
sections.translation: what is the strange plan of the Greeks that you know .

sections.label: Section 561
sections.original: φροῦδοι διώκοντές σε ναυτικῷ στόλῳ
sections.translation: Pursuers are on the way in search of you with a fleet . They are the aged Phoenix and the sons of Theseus .

sections.label: Section 563
sections.original: ὡς ἐκ βίας μʼ ἄξοντες ἢ λόγοις πάλιν ;
sections.translation: To bring me back by force , or through persuasion ?

sections.label: Section 564
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδʼ · ἀκούσας δʼ ἄγγελος πάρειμί σοι .
sections.translation: I do not know ; but I have come to tell you what I did hear .

sections.label: Section 565
sections.original: ἦ ταῦτα δὴ Φοῖνίξ τε χοἰ ξυνναυβάται
sections.translation: Are Phoenix and his comrades really so eager to do this favor for the Atreids ?

sections.label: Section 567
sections.original: ὡς ταῦτʼ ἐπίστω δρώμενʼ , οὐ μέλλοντʼ ἔτι .
sections.translation: Be sure that it is being done , and without delay .

sections.label: Section 568
sections.original: πῶς οὖν Ὀδυσσεὺς πρὸς τάδʼ οὐκ αὐτάγγελος
sections.translation: Then why was Odysseus not ready to sail on this mission , and bring the message himself ? Or did some fear hold him back ?

sections.label: Section 570
sections.original: κεῖνός γʼ ἐπʼ ἄλλον ἄνδρʼ ὁ Τυδέως τε παῖς
sections.translation: Oh , he and the son of Tydeus were readying for pursuit of another man , just as I was leaving port .

sections.label: Section 572
sections.original: πρὸς ποῖον αὖ τόνδʼ αὐτὸς Οὑδυσσεὺς ἔπλει ;
sections.translation: Who is this other after whom Odysseus himself was sailing ?

sections.label: Section 573
sections.original: ἦν δή τις — ἀλλὰ τόνδε μοι πρῶτον φράσον
sections.translation: There was a man . . . . But tell me first who that is over there . And whatever you say , do speak quietly .

sections.label: Section 575
sections.original: ὅδʼ ἔσθʼ ὁ κλεινός σοι Φιλοκτήτης , ξένε .
sections.translation: There , sir , before your eyes is the renowned Philoctetes .

sections.label: Section 576
sections.original: μή νύν μʼ ἔρῃ τὰ πλείονʼ , ἀλλʼ ὅσον τάχος
sections.translation: Then do not question me further , but get yourself out of here as quickly as possible and sail away .

sections.label: Section 578
sections.original: τί φησιν , ὦ παῖ ; τί με κατὰ σκότον ποτὲ
sections.translation: What is he saying , boy ? Why does the sailor traffic with you about me in these dark whispers ?

sections.label: Section 580
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδά πω τί φησι · δεῖ δʼ αὐτὸν λέγειν
sections.translation: His meaning yet eludes me . But , whatever he will say , he must say openly — to you , me and my men here .

sections.label: Section 582
sections.original: ὦ σπέρμʼ Ἀχιλλέως , μή με διαβάλῃς στρατῷ
sections.translation: Seed of Achilles , do not stir the army ’ s resentment against me for saying what I should not . I receive many benefits for what services I do them , as a poor man may .

sections.label: Section 585
sections.original: ἐγώ εἰμʼ Ἀτρείδαις δυσμενής · οὗτος δέ μοι
sections.translation: I am the enemy of the Atreids , and this man is my closest friend precisely because he hates them . Since , then , you have come kindly disposed towards me , you must not hide from us any part of their plans that you have heard .

sections.label: Section 589
sections.original: ὅρα τί ποιεῖς , παῖ .
sections.translation: Beware of what you are doing , boy .

sections.label: Section 589a
sections.original: σκοπῶ κἀγὼ πάλαι .
sections.translation: I am well aware .

sections.label: Section 590
sections.original: σὲ θήσομαι τῶνδʼ αἴτιον .
sections.translation: I will hold you accountable .

sections.label: Section 590a
sections.original: ποιοῦ λέγων .
sections.translation: Do so , but speak .

sections.label: Section 591
sections.original: λέγω . ʼπὶ τοῦτον ἄνδρε τώδʼ ὥπερ κλύεις ,
sections.translation: I will . It is after this man that those two whom I named to you , Diomedes and forceful Odysseus , are sailing . They are oath-bound to retrieve him , either by winning words or by overpowering might .

sections.label: Section 595
sections.original: καὶ ταῦτʼ Ἀχαιοὶ πάντες ἤκουον σαφῶς
sections.translation: And all the Achaeans heard this clearly from the mouth Odysseus , for his confidence of success in this action was higher than his comrade ’ s .

sections.label: Section 598
sections.original: τίνος δʼ Ἀτρεῖδαι τοῦδʼ ἄγαν οὕτω χρόνῳ
sections.translation: And for the sake of what did the Atreids after so long a time turn their thoughts so urgently towards this man ,

sections.label: Section 600
sections.original: ὅν γʼ εἶχον ἤδη χρόνιον ἐκβεβληκότες ;
sections.translation: whom they were long since keeping an outcast ? What was the desire that came over them , or what force ? What avenging spirit sent by the gods to exact payment for evil deeds ?

sections.label: Section 603
sections.original: ἐγώ σε τοῦτʼ , ἴσως γὰρ οὐκ ἀκήκοας ,
sections.translation: I will inform you of all that , since it seems that you have not heard . There was a seer of noble birth ,

sections.label: Section 605
sections.original: Πριάμου μὲν υἱός , ὄνομα δʼ ὠνομάζετο
sections.translation: a son of Priam , called Helenus , whom that man , out on a solitary night raid — that deceitful Odysseus , whose repute is all shame and dishonor — captured . Leading him back in bonds , he displayed him publicly to the Achaeans as his glorious prey .

sections.label: Section 610
sections.original: ὃς δὴ τά τʼ ἄλλʼ αὐτοῖσι πάντʼ ἐθέσπισεν
sections.translation: Helenus then prophesied for them whatever matter they asked , and , pertaining to Troy , he foretold that they would never sack its towers , unless by winning words they should bring Philoctetes here from the island where he now dwells . And , as soon as he heard the seer prophecy this , Laertes ’ son

sections.label: Section 615
sections.original: τὸν μάντιν εἰπόντʼ , εὐθέως ὑπέσχετο
sections.translation: immediately promised that he would bring the man and show him to the Achaeans . He thought it most likely that he would get him willingly , but , if unwilling , then by force , and he added that , were he to fail in this , whoever wished it might sever his head .

sections.label: Section 620
sections.original: ἤκουσας , ὦ παῖ , πάντα · τὸ σπεύδειν δέ σοι
sections.translation: You have heard everything , boy , and I advise speed for you , and for any man for whom you care .

sections.label: Section 622
sections.original: οἴμοι τάλας · ἦ κεῖνος , ἡ πᾶσα βλάβη ,
sections.translation: Alas ! Has he , the utter plague , sworn to fetch me back to the Achaeans by persuasion ? For if that were to happen , I could be persuaded , when dead , to come back up

sections.label: Section 625
sections.original: πρὸς φῶς ἀνελθεῖν , ὥσπερ οὑκείνου πατήρ .
sections.translation: from Hades into the light , as his father did !

sections.label: Section 626
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδʼ ἐγὼ ταῦτʼ · ἀλλʼ ἐγὼ μὲν εἶμʼ ἐπὶ
sections.translation: I know nothing about that . But for my part I must return to ship , while for you I pray that god may help you in every possible way . Exit Merchant .

sections.label: Section 628
sections.original: οὔκουν τάδʼ , ὦ παῖ , δεινά , τὸν Λαερτίου
sections.translation: Now is it not astounding , boy , that Odysseus would ever have expected by means of soft words

sections.label: Section 630
sections.original: δεῖξαι νεὼς ἄγοντʼ ἐν Ἀργείοις μέσοις ;
sections.translation: to lead me from his ship and show me in the middle of the Greeks ? No ! I would sooner listen to that greatest and worst of my enemies , the viper which made me crippled as I am ! But there is nothing that he would not say or dare . And now I know that he is coming here .

sections.label: Section 635
sections.original: ἀλλʼ , ὦ τέκνον , χωρῶμεν , ὡς ἡμᾶς πολὺ
sections.translation: Come , son , let us be moving , so that a wide sea may part us from the ship of Odysseus . Let us go ! Good speed in good season brings sleep and rest when toil is finished .

sections.label: Section 639
sections.original: οὐκοῦν ἐπειδὰν πνεῦμα τοὐκ πρῴρας ἀνῇ ,
sections.translation: Then as soon as the wind is not at our prow ,

sections.label: Section 640
sections.original: τότε στελοῦμεν · νῦν γὰρ ἀντιοστατεῖ .
sections.translation: we will sail . At present it blows against us .

sections.label: Section 641
sections.original: ἀεὶ καλὸς πλοῦς ἔσθʼ , ὅταν φεύγῃς κακά .
sections.translation: The sailing is always fair , when you flee trouble .
